params.text:
What is Safeguarding?

Safeguarding means protecting people's health, wellbeing, and human rights, enabling them to live free from harm, abuse, and neglect.

In this project:

  • Dignity: Always maintain patient dignity during photography
  • Autonomy: Respect patient choices and consent
  • Protection: Identify and respond to vulnerability
  • Professionalism: Maintain appropriate boundaries

Golden Rule: When in doubt about any safeguarding issue → STOP and consult.

Principle 1: Respect for Persons

  • Every patient has inherent worth and dignity
  • Treat all patients with respect regardless of background
  • Watch for non-verbal cues of discomfort
  • Allow breaks if needed
  • Have same-gender chaperone when appropriate

Principle 2: Informed Consent is Continuous

  • Consent is not a one-time checkbox
  • Patient can withdraw at any moment
  • Continuously assess comfort and willingness
  • Re-explain if you sense hesitation

Principle 3: Special Protection for Vulnerable

  • Children: Guardian consent + child assent
  • Disabilities: Extra time, simple explanations
  • Elderly: Gentle positioning, dignity maintenance
  • Marginalized: Awareness of stigma and power dynamics

Signs Consent May Not Be Voluntary:

Red FlagWhat It Might Mean
Patient seems confusedMay not understand what they're agreeing to
Looks to others for approvalMay be under pressure from family/authority
Appears fearful or intimidatedPower imbalance or coercion
Agrees too quicklyMay not have processed information
Body language shows reluctanceNon-verbal withdrawal of consent

If you see these signs:

  • Take more time to explain
  • Speak with patient privately
  • Ensure understanding
  • Confirm voluntary agreement
  • Consider declining to proceed

Scenario 1: Controlling Relationship

You are photographing a woman's back lesion. Her husband answers all questions before she can speak. When you ask her directly for consent, she looks at him first before nodding.

Concern: Possible lack of autonomous consent

Response:

  • Politely ask husband to step out
  • Speak with patient alone
  • Reassess consent without pressure
  • Watch for signs of fear or coercion
  • Follow institutional DV protocols if concerned

Scenario 2: Distressed Adolescent

Parent has consented for a 14-year-old's thigh lesion photo, but the teen keeps pulling clothing down and seems uncomfortable.

Response:

  • Stop immediately
  • Ask adolescent directly if they want to proceed
  • Honor their decision even if parent consented
  • Respect their bodily autonomy

If You Observe or Suspect:

  • Child abuse or neglect
  • Elder abuse
  • Domestic violence
  • Human trafficking
  • Sexual exploitation
  • Any serious safeguarding concern

You Must:

  • Follow your institution's reporting protocols
  • Report to appropriate authorities as required by law
  • Document observations
  • Ensure patient safety
  • Do NOT submit images showing signs of abuse

This is separate from the image collection project but is your professional duty.

Knowledge Check

A parent has consented for their 12-year-old child's photos, but the child tells you directly they don't want to be photographed. What should you do?

params.question: A parent has consented for their 12-year-old child's photos, but the child tells you directly they don't want to be photographed. What should you do?

answers.text: Proceed with the photography since the parent has legal authority
answers.tip: Incorrect. While parents have legal authority, children have bodily autonomy. Their objection should be respected.

answers.text: Stop and respect the child's wishes, even with parental consent
answers.tip: Correct! Assent from the child is essential. Their bodily autonomy and comfort take priority.

answers.text: Ask the parent to convince the child to participate
answers.tip: Incorrect. Pressuring a reluctant child violates their autonomy and could be traumatic.

answers.text: Proceed but take the photos very quickly
answers.tip: Incorrect. This completely disregards the child's clearly stated objection.
